title: Methods and apparatuses for processing blood
abstract: Systems and methods for the high yield extraction and recovery of higher quality target molecule(s) (e.g., DNA, RNA, protein, lipids, metabolites) from blood spots. High quality DNA recovered from dried blood spots can be an input source for high throughput analytical methods, such as for polymerase chain reaction (e.g., qPCR) and/or next generation sequencing (NGS). In various embodiments, at least 20.0 nanograms of nucleic acid (e.g., DNA, RNA), or at least 8.0 milligrams of protein, may be extracted and recovered per an amount of dried blood corresponding to approximately 5 microliters of fresh blood. In some embodiments, a majority (e.g., greater than 50%, greater than 60%, up to 95-100%) of the nucleic acid that is extracted and recovered from the blood spot via focused acoustics may be of a quality suitable for amplification via PCR or NGS.
claims:
Claims 1. A method of processing a dried blood spot, comprising: placing a sample in a vessel, the sample including blood dried on to a substrate; and transmitting focused acoustic energy through a wall of the vessel such that the blood and the substrate are exposed to acoustic energy having a frequency of between about 100 kilohertz and about 100 megahertz at a focal zone having a size dimension of less than about 2 centimeters, resulting in extraction of biomolecules from the blood, wherein at least 60% of nucleic acid of the extracted biomolecules is capable of amplification via polymerase chain reaction. 2. The method of claim 1 , wherein a greater percentage of nucleic acid extracted via the focused acoustic energy is capable of amplification via polymerase chain reaction than nucleic acid extracted without the focused acoustic energy. 3. The method of claim 1, wherein at least 80% of nucleic acid of the extracted biomolecules is capable of amplification via polymerase chain reaction. 4. The method of claim 1 , wherein at least 20.0 nanograms of nucleic acid of the extracted biomolecules per an amount of dried blood corresponding to 5 microliters of fresh blood is capable of amplification via polymerase chain reaction. 5. The method of claim 4, wherein between 20.0 nanograms and 160.0 nanograms of nucleic acid of the extracted biomolecules per an amount of dried blood corresponding to 5 microliters of fresh blood is capable of amplification via polymerase chain reaction. 6. The method of claim 1 , further comprising recovering from the extracted biomolecules at least 20.0 nanograms of nucleic acid per an amount of dried blood corresponding to 5 microliters of fresh blood. 7. The method of claim 1, further comprising recovering from the extracted biomolecules at least 8.0 milligrams of protein per an amount of dried blood corresponding to 5 microliters of fresh blood. 8. The method of claim 1, wherein the sample includes an amount of dried blood corresponding to less than 15 microliters of fresh blood. 9. The method of claim 1 , wherein transmitting focused acoustic energy through the wall of the vessel includes exposing the blood and the substrate to focused acoustic energy for less than 10 minutes. 10. The method of claim 9, transmitting focused acoustic energy through the wall of the vessel includes exposing the blood and the substrate to focused acoustic energy for between 2 minutes and 10 minutes. 11. The method of claim 1, wherein the substrate includes a porous synthetic polymer material. 12. The method of claim 11, wherein the porous synthetic polymer material includes at least one of polyethylene, polypropylene, polytetrafluoroethylene and polyethylene terephthalate. 13. The method of claim 1, wherein the substrate includes filter paper. 14. The method of claim 1, wherein exposure of the extracted biomolecules to focused acoustic energy causes fragmenting of nucleic acid from the extracted biomolecules to an average size of less than 1000 bp. 15. The method of claim 1, wherein the transmitting focused acoustic energy includes operating an acoustic transducer at a peak intensity power of at least 100 W. 16. The method of claim 1, wherein the transmitting focused acoustic energy includes operating an acoustic transducer at a duty factor of at least 10%. 17. The method of claim 1, wherein the transmitting focused acoustic energy includes operating an acoustic transducer at a cycles per burst of at least 100. 18. The method of claim 1, wherein the transmitting focused acoustic energy includes operating an acoustic transducer at a peak intensity power of between 100 W and 300 W, a duty factor of between 10% and 90% and a cycles per burst of between 100 and 300. 19. The method of claim 1, further comprising incubating the sample within the vessel at a temperature of between 20 degrees C and 60 degrees C. 20. The method of claim 19, further comprising incubating the sample within the vessel at a temperature of between 50 degrees C and 60 degrees C. 21. The method of claim 1, further comprising incubating the sample within the vessel at a temperature of between 20 degrees C and 40 degrees C. 22. A method of processing a dried blood spot, comprising: placing a sample in a vessel, the sample including dried blood on a substrate; transmitting focused acoustic energy through a wall of the vessel such that the blood and the substrate are exposed to acoustic energy having a frequency of between about 100 kilohertz and about 100 megahertz at a focal zone having a size dimension of less than about 2 centimeters, resulting in extraction of biomolecules from the blood; and recovering from the extracted biomolecules at least 20.0 nanograms of nucleic acid per an amount of dried blood corresponding to 5 microliters of fresh blood, or recovering from the extracted biomolecules at least 8.0 milligrams of protein per an amount of dried blood corresponding to 5 microliters of fresh blood. 23. The method of claim 22, wherein at least 60% of nucleic acid of the extracted biomolecules is capable of amplification via polymerase chain reaction. 24. The method of claim 22, wherein a greater percentage of nucleic acid extracted via the focused acoustic energy is capable of amplification via polymerase chain reaction than nucleic acid extracted without the focused acoustic energy. 25. The method of claim 22, wherein at least 20.0 nanograms of nucleic acid of the extracted biomolecules per an amount of dried blood corresponding to 5 microliters of fresh blood is capable of amplification via polymerase chain reaction. 26. The method of claim 22, wherein recovering from the extracted biomolecules includes recovering between 20.0 nanograms and 160.0 nanograms of nucleic acid per an amount of dried blood corresponding to 5 microliters of fresh blood. 27. The method of claim 22, wherein recovering from the extracted biomolecules includes recovering between 8.0 milligrams and 20.0 milligrams of protein per an amount of dried blood corresponding to 5 microliters of fresh blood. 28. The method of claim 22, wherein the sample includes an amount of dried blood corresponding to less than 15 microliters of fresh blood. 29. The method of claim 22, wherein transmitting focused acoustic energy through the wall of the vessel includes exposing the blood and the substrate to focused acoustic energy for less than 10 minutes. 30. The method of claim 22, wherein the substrate includes a porous synthetic polymer material. 31. The method of claim 22, wherein the transmitting focused acoustic energy includes operating an acoustic transducer at a duty factor of at least 10%. 34. The method of claim 22, wherein the transmitting focused acoustic energy includes operating an acoustic transducer at a cycles per burst of at least 100. 35. The method of claim 22, wherein the transmitting focused acoustic energy includes operating an acoustic transducer at a peak intensity power of between 100 W and 300 W, a duty factor of between 10% and 90% and a cycles per burst of between 100 and 300. 36. The method of claim 22, further comprising incubating the sample within the vessel at a temperature of between 20 degrees C and 60 degrees C. 37. The method of claim 36, further comprising incubating the sample within the vessel at a temperature of between 50 degrees C and 60 degrees C. 38. The method of claim 36, further comprising incubating the sample within the vessel at a temperature of between 20 degrees C and 40 degrees C. 39. A method of processing a dried blood spot, comprising: placing a sample in a vessel, the sample including blood dried on to a substrate; and transmitting focused acoustic energy through a wall of the vessel such that the blood and the substrate are exposed to acoustic energy having a frequency of between about 100 kilohertz and about 100 megahertz at a focal zone having a size dimension of less than about 2 centimeters, resulting in extraction of biomolecules from the blood, wherein a greater percentage of nucleic acid extracted from the sample via the focused acoustic energy is capable of amplification via polymerase chain reaction than nucleic acid extracted from an identical sample using extraction protocol from a QIAamp DNA Mini Kit without the focused acoustic energy. 40. The method of claim 39, wherein greater than 20% of the nucleic acid extracted from the sample via the focused acoustic energy is capable of amplification 

 via polymerase chain reaction than nucleic acid extracted from an identical sample using extraction protocol from a QIAamp DNA Mini Kit without the focused acoustic energy. 41. The method of claim 39, wherein at least 60% of nucleic acid of the extracted biomolecules is capable of amplification via polymerase chain reaction. 42. The method of claim 39, further comprising recovering from the extracted biomolecules at least 20.0 nanograms of nucleic acid per an amount of dried blood corresponding to 5 microliters of fresh blood. 43. The method of claim 42, wherein recovering from the extracted biomolecules includes recovering between 20.0 nanograms and 160.0 nanograms of nucleic acid per an amount of dried blood corresponding to 5 microliters of fresh blood. 44. The method of claim 39, further comprising recovering from the extracted biomolecules at least 8.0 milligrams of protein per an amount of dried blood corresponding to 5 microliters of fresh blood. 45. The method of claim 44, wherein recovering from the extracted biomolecules includes recovering between 8.0 milligrams and 20.0 milligrams of protein per an amount of dried blood corresponding to 5 microliters of fresh blood. 46. The method of claim 39, wherein the sample includes an amount of dried blood corresponding to less than 15 microliters of fresh blood. 47. The method of claim 39, wherein transmitting focused acoustic energy through the wall of the vessel includes exposing the blood and the substrate to focused acoustic energy for less than 10 minutes. 48. The method of claim 39, wherein the substrate includes a porous synthetic polymer material. 49. The method of claim 39, wherein exposure of the extracted biomolecules to focused acoustic energy causes fragmenting of nucleic acid from the extracted biomolecules to an average size of less than 1000 bp. 50. The method of claim 39, wherein the transmitting focused acoustic energy includes operating an acoustic transducer at a peak intensity power of at least 100 W. 51. The method of claim 39, wherein the transmitting focused acoustic energy includes operating an acoustic transducer at a duty factor of at least 10%. 52. The method of claim 39, wherein the transmitting focused acoustic energy includes operating an acoustic transducer at a peak intensity power of between 100 W and 300 W, a duty factor of between 10% and 90% and a cycles per burst of between 100 and 300. 54.
